config.title: Lippencott Pathology Ch16 (JeffMD Block 5 - Renal)
config.subtitle: Renal pathology: cystic diseases, glomerulonephritides, nephrotic/nephritic syndromes, tubular/interstitial disorders, vascular diseases, and renal neoplasms

questions.stem: The mother of a 2-month-old child palpates a mass on the left side of the child's abdomen. Vital signs are normal. A CT-guided renal biopsy shows undifferentiated tubules surrounded by undifferentiated mesenchyme, smooth muscle, and islands of cartilage. The mass is removed and displays variably sized cysts. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this child's flank mass?
questions.explanation: Renal dysplasia is characterized by undifferentiated tubular structures surrounded by primitive mesenchyme, sometimes with heterotopic tissue such as smooth muscle and cartilage. Cysts often form from the abnormal tubules, resulting from an abnormality in metanephric differentiation. Variants include multicystic forms (seen here), which present as a palpable flank mass shortly after birth and represent the most common cause of an abdominal mass in newborns. Infantile polycystic disease (choice A) is invariably bilateral with very large kidneys. Medullary sponge kidney (choice B) is characterized by small cysts limited to the renal papillae. Wilms tumor (choice E) may contain heterologous elements but does not form large variably sized cysts. Neuroblastoma (choice C) arises from adrenal/neural tissue, not kidney parenchyma.
keypoints: ⭐ Renal dysplasia = undifferentiated tubules + primitive mesenchyme ± heterotopic elements (smooth muscle, cartilage) → variably sized cysts
Multicystic renal dysplasia is the most common cause of an abdominal mass in neonates
Results from abnormal metanephric differentiation during embryogenesis
Distinguish from ARPKD (bilateral, no heterotopic tissue) and Wilms tumor (no large cysts)
questions.imageAlt: Gross pathology specimen showing a multicystic renal mass with variably sized cysts
questions.imageCaption: Resected multicystic renal mass displaying variably sized cysts — characteristic of multicystic renal dysplasia

questions.stem: A 38-year-old man presents with vague flank pain and describes the passage of blood clots in his urine. Physical examination reveals bilateral flank and abdominal masses. Laboratory studies show elevated blood urea nitrogen and creatinine. Urinalysis reveals hematuria, proteinuria, and oliguria. A CT scan discloses bilaterally, massively enlarged kidneys. The patient subsequently develops end-stage kidney disease and receives a renal transplant. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Autosomal dominant polycystic kidney disease (ADPKD) is characterized by bilaterally enlarged multicystic kidneys, and is the most common hereditary cystic renal disease. Most cases are caused by mutations in PKD1 (polycystin). Symptoms typically manifest in the fourth decade of life and include flank pain, bilateral abdominal masses, hematuria, and azotemia that progresses to uremia in approximately half of patients. Autosomal recessive PKD (choice B) presents in infants, not adults. Hydronephrosis (choice C) does not feature multiple cysts. Medullary sponge kidney (choice D) consists of multiple small cysts limited to the medulla. Multicystic renal dysplasia (choice E) is typically unilateral.
keypoints: ⭐ ADPKD: AD inheritance, PKD1 (polycystin) mutation, bilateral massively enlarged cystic kidneys presenting in 4th decade
50% of ADPKD patients develop end-stage renal failure; also associated with hepatic cysts, intracranial aneurysms
ARPKD = infants; ADPKD = adults — key distinction
Symptoms: flank pain, hematuria (blood clots), hypertension, palpable bilateral masses
questions.imageAlt: Gross pathology showing bilaterally massively enlarged kidneys with numerous cortical cysts
questions.imageCaption: Surgically resected kidneys demonstrating bilateral massive cystic enlargement consistent with ADPKD

questions.stem: The patient described in the previous question (38-year-old man with autosomal dominant polycystic kidney disease who underwent renal transplant) carries an increased risk for which of the following abnormalities?
choices: Transitional cell carcinoma of the bladder
questions.explanation: One third of patients with autosomal dominant polycystic kidney disease (ADPKD) also develop hepatic cysts, whose lining resembles bile duct epithelium. This is the most important extrarenal manifestation alongside intracranial (berry) aneurysms. Horseshoe kidney (choice B) is a developmental fusion anomaly unrelated to ADPKD. Pulmonary cysts (choice C), renal cell carcinoma (choice D), and transitional cell carcinoma (choice E) do not arise as direct complications of ADPKD.
keypoints: ⭐ ADPKD extrarenal manifestations: hepatic cysts (most common), intracranial berry aneurysms (risk of subarachnoid hemorrhage), mitral valve prolapse
Hepatic cysts in ADPKD are lined by biliary epithelium; usually asymptomatic
~10% of ADPKD patients have intracranial aneurysms — most feared complication
Screen ADPKD patients for intracranial aneurysms with MRA if family history of rupture

questions.stem: A 46-year-old woman presents with a 6-month history of vague upper abdominal pain after fatty meals, some abdominal distension, and frequent indigestion. Physical examination shows an obese woman (BMI = 32 kg/m²) with right upper quadrant tenderness. A CT scan discloses gallstones and an ectopic kidney. Which of the following is the expected location of the ectopic kidney?
choices: Attached to the left adrenal gland
Fused laterally with the contralateral kidney
questions.explanation: Most ectopic kidneys are located along the pathway of normal renal ascent during fetal development and are caudal to their normal lumbar position. During fetal life, the kidneys are initially located in the lower abdomen. As development progresses, they migrate upward toward the lumbar retroperitoneum. Kidneys that fail to complete this ascent and remain in the pelvis or presacral area are termed ectopic. Fusion of both kidneys at their lower poles results in a horseshoe kidney, not an ectopic kidney. The other locations listed are not consistent with the known pathway of renal migration.
keypoints: ⭐ Ectopic kidney = failure of normal cranial migration during fetal development → most commonly found in the pelvis
Pelvic kidneys may be clinically silent or discovered incidentally; may cause obstruction or infections
Horseshoe kidney = fusion of lower poles + failure to ascend past the inferior mesenteric artery
Distinguish ectopic (wrong location) from horseshoe (fusion anomaly)

questions.stem: A 12-year-old girl complains of swelling of her eyelids, abdomen, and ankles. She had been in good health until several months ago, when she gained some weight and noted swelling of her lower legs. An X-ray film of the chest shows bilateral pleural effusions, without evidence of lung disease. Urinalysis reveals heavy proteinuria (8 g per 24 hours) without hematuria. A percutaneous needle biopsy of the kidney discloses no morphologic abnormalities by light microscopy. Which of the following best describes this patient's medical condition?
questions.explanation: This patient has minimal change glomerulopathy presenting as nephrotic syndrome. Nephrotic syndrome is characterized by heavy proteinuria (>3.5 g per 24 hours), hypoalbuminemia, hyperlipidemia, and edema. In minimal change glomerulopathy, there is effacement of podocyte foot processes (visible only on electron microscopy), allowing massive protein loss into the urine. Light microscopy shows no morphologic abnormalities, as seen here. The question asks for the best description of the patient's medical condition overall, which is nephrotic syndrome. The other choices represent specific diagnoses with distinct morphologic findings on biopsy.
keypoints: ⭐ Nephrotic syndrome = proteinuria >3.5 g/day + hypoalbuminemia + edema + hyperlipidemia ± lipiduria
Minimal change disease (MCD): most common cause of nephrotic syndrome in children; normal light microscopy; podocyte foot process effacement on EM only
MCD responds well to corticosteroids; does not progress to renal failure
Edema in nephrotic syndrome: low oncotic pressure from hypoalbuminemia → fluid shifts to interstitium → periorbital, peripheral, and abdominal edema + pleural effusions

questions.stem: An 8-year-old boy presents with headaches, dizziness, and malaise. He was seen for a severe sore throat 2 weeks ago. Physical examination reveals facial edema. The blood pressure is 180/110 mm Hg. A 24-hour urine collection demonstrates oliguria, and urinalysis shows hematuria. Which of the following best describes this patient's medical condition?
questions.explanation: This is a classic presentation of nephritic syndrome in the setting of poststreptococcal glomerulonephritis. The key features are: sore throat 2 weeks prior (from group A β-hemolytic streptococci), followed by hematuria, hypertension, oliguria, edema, and elevated BUN/creatinine. Nephritic syndrome results from inflammatory changes in glomeruli (leukocyte infiltration, mesangial/endothelial cell hyperplasia), and is characterized by hematuria, variable proteinuria, oliguria, and hypertension. The 2-week latent period after pharyngitis is classic for poststreptococcal GN. The other choices are not triggered by streptococcal pharyngitis, and membranous GN (choice B) and minimal change (choice C) do not present with hematuria.
keypoints: ⭐ Nephritic syndrome = hematuria + hypertension + oliguria + variable proteinuria; caused by glomerular inflammation
Poststreptococcal GN: 2-week latency after pharyngitis (or 4-6 weeks after skin infection) with group A β-hemolytic strep
Contrast with nephrotic syndrome: nephrotic = heavy proteinuria, edema, no hematuria; nephritic = hematuria, HTN, oliguria
Most children with postinfectious GN recover completely without progression to renal failure

questions.stem: What finding on microscopic urinalysis indicates that hematuria in the patient described in Question 6 (8-year-old boy with poststreptococcal glomerulonephritis) is caused by a renal process, rather than bleeding from another site in the urinary tract?
questions.explanation: Red blood cell (RBC) casts in the urine sediment are pathognomonic of a glomerular or renal tubular source of hematuria. They form when erythrocytes are compacted during passage through the renal tubules and become embedded in Tamm-Horsfall protein. Their presence confirms glomerulonephritis as the source of bleeding, distinguishing it from lower urinary tract causes (bladder, urethra, prostate) which do not produce casts. Blood clots (choice A) suggest lower urinary tract bleeding. Hemoglobin crystals (choice B) and phagocytosed hemoglobin (choice C) do not localize hematuria to the kidney. White blood cell casts (choice E) indicate renal infection (pyelonephritis), not glomerulonephritis.
keypoints: ⭐ RBC casts = glomerulonephritis — pathognomonic for renal source of hematuria
WBC casts = pyelonephritis or interstitial nephritis
Granular/muddy brown casts = acute tubular necrosis
Hyaline casts = normal or nephrotic syndrome; waxy casts = chronic renal failure
Lower urinary tract hematuria (bladder/prostate/urethra): no casts, often terminal hematuria or gross clots

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man complains of chronic back pain and fatigue, excessive urination, and increased thirst. X-ray examination reveals numerous lytic lesions in the lumbar vertebral bodies. Laboratory studies show hypoalbuminemia, mild anemia, and thrombocytopenia. Urinalysis displays 4+ proteinuria. A monoclonal immunoglobulin light-chain peak is demonstrated by serum electrophoresis. A bone marrow biopsy discloses foci of plasma cells accounting for 20% of all hematopoietic cells. Which of the following is the most likely cause of nephrotic syndrome in this patient?
questions.explanation: The clinicopathologic findings establish multiple myeloma (lytic bone lesions, monoclonal light-chain peak, >20% plasma cells in marrow, anemia, thrombocytopenia). Amyloid nephropathy in this context is caused by deposition of secreted lambda or kappa light chains (AL amyloid) in the glomerular basement membranes and mesangial matrix, producing amorphous acellular material that obstructs glomerular capillaries and causes nephrotic-range proteinuria. Deposits of AL amyloid also appear in tubular basement membranes and vessel walls. The nodular pattern of amyloid deposition can resemble Kimmelstiel-Wilson nodules (choice E), but amyloid is NOT PAS-positive and IS identifiable by Congo red staining with apple-green birefringence. IgA nephropathy (choice C) and membranous GN (choice D) are unrelated to light-chain disease.
keypoints: ⭐ Multiple myeloma → AL amyloidosis → amyloid deposits in GBM and mesangium → nephrotic syndrome
Congo red stain with apple-green birefringence under polarized light = diagnostic for amyloid
AL amyloid = light chains (Ig); AA amyloid = serum amyloid A (from chronic inflammation)
Amyloid nephropathy presents as nephrotic syndrome and may cause hepatosplenomegaly and cardiac involvement
Distinguish from Kimmelstiel-Wilson (diabetic nodular sclerosis): PAS-positive, Congo red-negative
questions.imageAlt: Renal biopsy showing amorphous acellular material expanding the mesangium (amyloid deposition)
questions.imageCaption: Renal biopsy with amorphous mesangial deposits — amyloid nephropathy (AL type) in the setting of multiple myeloma

questions.stem: A 12-year-old boy complains of swelling of his feet for the past 3 weeks. He is otherwise healthy, with no known previous illness. Vital signs are normal. Physical examination reveals pitting edema of the lower legs and a swollen abdomen. Urinalysis shows 4+ protein but no RBCs or WBCs. Which of the following are the most likely diagnoses to consider in your evaluation of this patient?
choices: Minimal change disease, focal segmental glomerulosclerosis
questions.explanation: A 12-year-old with nephrotic-range proteinuria (4+ protein), edema, and no hematuria or pyuria most likely has minimal change disease (MCD) or focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (FSGS). MCD causes 90% of nephrotic syndrome in young children and is characterized by podocyte foot process effacement on EM, with no light microscopic changes. FSGS causes nephrotic syndrome in 10% of children and 30% of adults, and is more often steroid-resistant. Henoch-Schönlein purpura and lupus (choice A) present with nephritic syndrome features including hematuria and rash. Malignant hypertension and renal vein thrombosis (choice B) are not typical in healthy children. Pyelonephritis and ATN (choice D) present with pyuria, RBC/WBC casts, and systemic infection. Wilms tumor and RCC (choice E) are neoplastic, not causes of nephrotic syndrome.
keypoints: ⭐ Nephrotic syndrome in a child without hematuria → first consider MCD (corticosteroid-responsive) then FSGS (often steroid-resistant)
MCD = #1 cause of nephrotic syndrome in children; FSGS = #1 cause in adults (and HIV-associated nephropathy)
No RBCs, no WBCs in nephrotic syndrome — their presence suggests nephritic component
MCD responds completely to steroids; FSGS often progresses to ESRD

questions.stem: A 4-year-old girl presents with swelling of the legs and ankles. Physical examination reveals pitting edema of the lower extremities. Urinalysis shows 2+ proteinuria. The urinary sediment contains no inflammatory cells or red blood cells. Serum levels of BUN and creatinine are normal. The patient recovers completely after a course of corticosteroids. Which of the following pathologic findings might be expected in the urine prior to treatment with corticosteroids?
questions.explanation: This child has minimal change disease (lipoid nephrosis), confirmed by the complete response to corticosteroids. The loss of albumin in the urine leads to hypoalbuminemia, which triggers compensatory hepatic lipoprotein synthesis and hyperlipidemia. Lipid droplets (free fat, oval fat bodies, or fatty casts) appear in the urine as a direct consequence of this hyperlipidemia, lending the classic name 'lipoid nephrosis.' Eosinophils (choice B) appear in allergic (drug-induced) interstitial nephritis. RBC casts (choice D) indicate glomerulonephritis. WBC casts (choice E) are a feature of pyelonephritis. Amyloid casts (choice A) are not a recognized urinary finding.
keypoints: ⭐ Minimal change disease (lipoid nephrosis): nephrotic syndrome + lipid droplets/oval fat bodies in urine + hyperlipidemia + hypoalbuminemia
Lipid droplets in urine = result of hyperlipidemia caused by compensatory hepatic lipoprotein synthesis in response to hypoalbuminemia
Complete response to corticosteroids is characteristic of MCD; steroid-resistance should prompt consideration of FSGS
Eosinophils in urine → allergic/drug-induced interstitial nephritis (e.g., NSAIDs, penicillin)

questions.stem: For the patient described in the previous question (4-year-old girl with minimal change disease), electron microscopy of a renal biopsy specimen prior to treatment would most likely demonstrate which of the following abnormalities?
choices: Electron-dense immune deposits in the capillary basement membranes
Electron-dense immune deposits in the mesangium
Loss of microvilli by the tubular lining cells
questions.explanation: The hallmark finding of minimal change disease on electron microscopy is complete effacement (fusion) of visceral epithelial cell (podocyte) foot processes. This retraction of the foot processes disrupts the slit diaphragm, the main barrier to protein filtration, allowing massive proteinuria. Light microscopy is essentially normal in MCD. MCD does not feature immune complex deposits (choices B and C), which are seen in membranous nephropathy and IgA nephropathy, respectively. Basement membrane duplication (choice A) is seen in membranoproliferative GN. Loss of tubular microvilli (choice E) is not the primary pathologic finding in MCD, which is a glomerular, not tubular, disease.
keypoints: ⭐ Minimal change disease EM finding: effacement (fusion) of podocyte foot processes — the key ultrastructural abnormality
Light microscopy: normal; IF: negative (no immune deposits); EM: foot process effacement
Podocyte foot process fusion disrupts the slit diaphragm → loss of filtration barrier → selective proteinuria (albumin > globulins)
Foot process effacement is not specific to MCD — it occurs in all nephrotic-range proteinuria, but is characteristic

questions.stem: A 44-year-old man complains of swelling of his legs and puffiness around his eyes. His abdomen has become protuberant and he feels short of breath. Physical examination reveals generalized edema and ascites. Total serum protein is 5.2 g/dL, and albumin is 1.9 g/dL. Serum cholesterol is elevated at 530 mg/dL. There are 5 g of protein in a 24-hour urine collection. The urinary sediment contains many hyalin casts but no RBCs or inflammatory cells. A renal biopsy stained by direct immunofluorescence for IgG shows granular deposits outlining glomerular capillary loops. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Membranous glomerulopathy is a frequent cause of nephrotic syndrome in adults and is caused by accumulation of immune complexes in the subepithelial zone of glomerular capillaries. The characteristic immunofluorescence pattern is granular deposits of IgG outlining the glomerular capillary loops, as described in this biopsy. The clinical presentation — adult with nephrotic syndrome (5 g proteinuria, hypoalbuminemia, hyperlipidemia, edema) and no hematuria — is classic. Amyloid nephropathy (choice A) stains with Congo red, not IgG. FSGS (choice B) shows segmental sclerosis on light microscopy. MPGN type I (choice C) features subendothelial immune complex deposits and mesangial proliferation. Minimal change disease (choice E) shows no immunofluorescence deposits.
keypoints: ⭐ Membranous glomerulopathy: granular IgG deposits along glomerular capillary loops (subepithelial) on IF — 'spike and dome' pattern on EM
Most common cause of nephrotic syndrome in adults (non-diabetic); often idiopathic or secondary (SLE, hepatitis B, solid tumors, penicillamine)
Outcomes: spontaneous remission ~25%, stable with partial loss of function ~50%, progressive renal failure ~25%
Distinguish from MPGN: MPGN has mesangial proliferation and subendothelial deposits; membranous has no cellularity, subepithelial deposits
questions.imageAlt: Direct immunofluorescence for IgG showing granular deposits outlining glomerular capillary loops
questions.imageCaption: Direct immunofluorescence (IgG): granular peripheral capillary loop deposits — membranous glomerulopathy

questions.stem: The pathogenesis of nephrotic syndrome in the patient described in Question 12 (44-year-old man with membranous glomerulopathy) is best characterized by which of the following mechanisms of disease?
choices: Deposition of antiglomerular basement membrane antibody
Deposition of IgA in the mesangium
Expansion of the glomerular basement membrane with PAS-positive glycoproteins
questions.explanation: Membranous glomerulopathy is caused by localization of immune complexes in the subepithelial zone (between the visceral epithelial cell/podocyte and the glomerular basement membrane), either via in situ formation or deposition of circulating immune complexes. This subepithelial location separates them from the inflammatory cells in the capillary lumen, explaining why membranous GN causes nephrotic (not nephritic) syndrome — there is protein loss without inflammatory cell infiltration or hematuria. Antiglomerular basement membrane antibody deposition (choice A) is the mechanism in Goodpasture syndrome, causing a linear IF pattern. IgA in the mesangium (choice B) is the mechanism of Berger disease. PAS-positive GBM expansion (choice C) reflects diabetic glomerulosclerosis. Subendothelial immune complex deposits (choice D) are seen in lupus nephritis and MPGN.
keypoints: ⭐ Membranous nephropathy: SUBEPITHELIAL immune complex deposits → granular IF → 'spike and dome' on EM → nephrotic syndrome (no inflammation access)
Subendothelial deposits → close to inflammatory cells → nephritic syndrome (lupus nephritis, MPGN)
Anti-PLA2R antibody = most common cause of primary (idiopathic) membranous nephropathy
Location of deposits determines clinical syndrome: subepithelial = nephrotic; subendothelial/mesangial = nephritic

questions.stem: The glomerular changes found in the patient described in Question 12 (membranous glomerulopathy) are frequently seen in patients with which of the following systemic diseases?
questions.explanation: Immune complexes formed against DNA, RNA, and autologous proteins in systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) may be deposited along the glomerular basement membrane in a pattern indistinguishable from idiopathic membranous nephropathy (Class V lupus nephritis). However, membranous nephropathy of lupus also features mesangial and subendothelial deposits of immunoglobulins, unlike idiopathic membranous. Goodpasture syndrome (choice B) causes linear anti-GBM antibody deposits, not granular subepithelial deposits. Scleroderma (choice C) causes vascular and interstitial renal disease, not membranous GN. Amyloid nephropathy (choice A) and Wegener granulomatosis (choice E) are not associated with membranous glomerulopathy.
keypoints: ⭐ Secondary membranous nephropathy associations: SLE (Class V), hepatitis B, solid tumors (paraneoplastic), drugs (penicillamine, NSAIDs, gold)
SLE lupus nephritis classes: I=minimal, II=mesangial, III=focal proliferative, IV=diffuse proliferative (worst), V=membranous
Class V lupus nephritis = membranous pattern + mesangial/subendothelial deposits (distinguishes from idiopathic membranous)
Always consider secondary causes in membranous GN: check ANA, anti-dsDNA, hepatitis serology, and age-appropriate cancer screening

questions.stem: A 14-year-old girl presents with a 5-day history of hypertension, oliguria, and hematuria. She was seen 2 weeks earlier for a severe throat infection with group A (β-hemolytic) streptococci. A kidney biopsy displays glomerulonephritis. Immunofluorescence staining for which of the following proteins would provide the strongest evidence that this patient's glomerulonephritis is mediated by immune complexes?
questions.explanation: In acute postinfectious glomerulonephritis, immune complexes localize in glomeruli by deposition from the circulation or by in situ formation as bacterial antigens bind circulating antibodies. The renal biopsy characteristically shows complement fixation. Complement activation is so extensive that over 90% of patients develop hypocomplementemia (decreased serum C3) during the acute phase. Complement (C3, C1q, C4) staining by immunofluorescence directly demonstrates immune complex activation, strongly supporting an immune-complex-mediated mechanism. Fibrinogen (choice B), Hageman factor (choice C), plasminogen (choice D), and thrombin (choice E) are all components of the coagulation cascade, not immune complexes, and their deposition would not indicate immune complex–mediated disease.
keypoints: ⭐ Poststreptococcal GN: immune complexes activate complement → C3 depletion → hypocomplementemia (C3 ↓) in >90% of cases
IF in poststreptococcal GN: granular deposits of IgG and C3 along capillary walls and in mesangium ('lumpy-bumpy' pattern)
EM: subepithelial 'humps' + mesangial and subendothelial deposits
C3 normalizes within 6-8 weeks; persistent hypocomplementemia suggests MPGN or lupus

questions.stem: A 28-year-old man complains of nasal obstruction, bloody nose, cough, and bloody sputum. A chest X-ray displays cavitated lesions and multiple nodules within both lung fields. Urinalysis reveals 3+ hematuria and red blood cell casts. Laboratory studies show anemia and elevated serum levels of C-ANCA (antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibody). Peripheral eosinophils are not increased. A renal biopsy exhibits focal glomerular necrosis with crescents and vasculitis affecting arterioles and venules. What is the appropriate diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Wegener granulomatosis (granulomatosis with polyangiitis) is a systemic necrotizing vasculitis characterized by granulomatous lesions of the upper respiratory tract (nose, sinuses) and lungs, associated with glomerular disease. More than 90% of patients have ANCA, of whom 75% have C-ANCA (cytoplasmic pattern, anti-PR3). Renal biopsy shows focal necrotizing glomerulonephritis with crescents and vasculitis affecting small vessels. Churg-Strauss syndrome (choice A) features eosinophilia and asthma — peripheral eosinophils are NOT increased here. Goodpasture syndrome (choice B) presents with pulmonary hemorrhage and GN but involves anti-GBM antibodies and linear IF, without granulomas. Polyarteritis nodosa (choice D) spares the lungs. Hypersensitivity vasculitis (choice C) does not cause cavitary lung lesions or C-ANCA.
keypoints: ⭐ Wegener (GPA): upper airway granulomas + lung cavitations + crescentic GN + C-ANCA (anti-PR3) — classic triad
ANCA: C-ANCA (anti-PR3) = Wegener; P-ANCA (anti-MPO) = microscopic polyangiitis, Churg-Strauss
Churg-Strauss (EGPA): asthma + eosinophilia + P-ANCA — distinguish by eosinophilia and asthma
Goodpasture: anti-GBM antibodies → linear IF; Wegener: immune complexes → granular/pauci-immune IF

questions.stem: Which of the following best describes the renal disease of the patient described in Question 16 (28-year-old man with Wegener granulomatosis)?
questions.explanation: Focal necrotizing glomerulonephritis is one of the early features of Wegener granulomatosis. ANCA autoantibodies activate neutrophils, causing them to adhere to endothelial cells, release toxic oxygen metabolites, degranulate, and kill endothelial cells. This disrupts the glomerular basement membrane, leading to extravasation of inflammatory cells into the urinary space and crescent formation. Clinically, the disease presents as rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis (RPGN), a clinical syndrome denoting rapid-onset renal failure due to severe glomerular injury (often defined as doubling of creatinine within weeks). Crescentic GN is the morphologic correlate of RPGN. Wegener does not cause membranoproliferative GN (choices D, E), and nephrotic syndrome (choice B) is not the presentation here (hematuria and RBC casts indicate nephritic pathology).
keypoints: ⭐ RPGN (rapidly progressive GN) = rapid loss of renal function over days-weeks; morphologic correlate = CRESCENTIC GN (>50% crescents)
Crescents = proliferating parietal epithelial cells + macrophages filling Bowman's space after GBM rupture
Three RPGN types: Type I = anti-GBM (Goodpasture, linear IF); Type II = immune complex (SLE, postinfectious, granular IF); Type III = pauci-immune (ANCA-associated: Wegener, MPA, Churg-Strauss)
Treatment: high-dose steroids + cyclophosphamide for ANCA-associated RPGN

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man with a history of drug addiction presents with a 6-month history of progressive swelling in his ankles and abdomen. Urinalysis shows heavy proteinuria (>4 g per 24 hours) but no evidence of inflammatory cells or RBCs. Laboratory studies reveal hyperlipidemia and hypoalbuminemia. Serum creatinine level is normal. The blood test for ANCA is negative. The patient responds well to corticosteroids, but edema and proteinuria recur the following year. Upon the third recurrence, the patient becomes steroid resistant. A renal biopsy shows segmental obliteration of capillary loops by collagen and lipid accumulation in some, but not all, glomeruli. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient's glomerulopathy?
questions.explanation: Focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (FSGS) is characterized by glomerular scarring (sclerosis) that affects some (focal), but not all, glomeruli and initially involves only part of an affected glomerular tuft (segmental). By light microscopy, varying numbers of glomeruli show segmental obliteration of capillary loops by increased collagen and accumulation of lipid or proteinaceous material. FSGS is the most common renal complication of intravenous drug abuse and is the cause of nephrotic syndrome in 30% of adults. The clinical course of initially steroid-responsive but ultimately steroid-resistant nephrotic syndrome is characteristic of FSGS. HIV nephropathy is a severe collapsing variant of FSGS. The other choices involve glomeruli diffusely or have different morphologic features.
keypoints: ⭐ FSGS: FOCAL (some glomeruli) + SEGMENTAL (part of tuft) sclerosis → collagen + lipid in capillary loops
Associated with: IV drug abuse (heroin nephropathy), HIV (collapsing FSGS), obesity, reflux nephropathy, podocin/nephrin mutations
Steroid-resistant nephrotic syndrome + IV drug use → think FSGS
FSGS is #1 cause of nephrotic syndrome in adults and most common cause in African Americans; often progresses to ESRD
questions.imageAlt: Renal biopsy showing segmental sclerosis obliterating capillary loops in some glomeruli with others appearing normal
questions.imageCaption: Light microscopy: focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (FSGS) — segmental capillary loop obliteration by collagen and lipid

questions.stem: A 20-year-old woman is involved in an automobile accident and loses a large amount of blood. In response to hypoxia, interstitial peritubular cells of the kidney would be expected to release which of the following hormones?
questions.explanation: Erythropoietin (EPO) is released by interstitial peritubular cells (fibroblast-like cells in the renal cortex) in response to hypoxia. EPO activates specific receptors on erythroid progenitor cells in the bone marrow, rescuing them from apoptosis, promoting colony growth, and restoring normal red blood cell mass. Renin (choice E) is released by the juxtaglomerular (JG) cells of the afferent arteriole in response to decreased renal perfusion pressure — a distinct cell type and location. Aldosterone (choice A) is produced by the adrenal cortex, not the kidney. Angiotensin (choice B) is generated from angiotensinogen by renin in the bloodstream. Angiotensinogen (choice C) is synthesized by the liver.
keypoints: ⭐ EPO = released by peritubular interstitial cells of renal cortex in response to HYPOXIA → stimulates erythropoiesis
Renin = released by JG cells of afferent arteriole in response to ↓ renal perfusion pressure, ↓ Na+ delivery to macula densa, or sympathetic stimulation
CKD → loss of EPO-producing cells → normocytic normochromic anemia of chronic kidney disease
Treatment: recombinant EPO (epoetin alfa, darbepoetin) for anemia of CKD

questions.stem: A 32-year-old man complains of recurrent hematuria since his youth. The hematuria typically occurs following upper respiratory tract infections. Vital signs are normal. Urinalysis shows proteinuria, hematuria, and a few red blood cell casts. Laboratory studies disclose normal levels of BUN and creatinine. The ANA and ANCA tests are negative. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Berger disease (IgA nephropathy) is the most common form of glomerulonephritis in adults. It presents with recurrent gross or microscopic hematuria that is typically triggered by mucosal infections (URI or GI) — a feature called 'synpharyngitic hematuria' (hematuria occurs during or immediately after the infection, unlike the 2-week latency of poststreptococcal GN). Deposition of IgA-dominant immune complexes in the mesangium is the cause of nephropathy. ANA and ANCA are negative. Amyloid nephropathy (choice A) and membranous glomerulopathy (choice D) do not feature RBC casts. Alport syndrome (choice C) also causes hematuria from birth, but involves abnormal type IV collagen and is associated with hearing and visual defects. Wegener (choice E) is ANCA-positive.
keypoints: ⭐ IgA nephropathy (Berger disease): recurrent hematuria + synpharyngitic timing (during/after URI) + mesangial IgA deposits
Most common GN worldwide; predominantly affects males 20s-30s
Distinguish from poststreptococcal GN: IgA = hematuria DURING infection; poststrep GN = hematuria 2 weeks AFTER infection
20% of IgA nephropathy patients progress to renal failure over 10 years; treatment = RAAS blockade ± immunosuppression

questions.stem: For the patient described in Question 20 (32-year-old man with IgA nephropathy), which of the following patterns of IgA immunofluorescence would be expected in the renal biopsy?
questions.explanation: IgA nephropathy (Berger disease) is caused by immune complexes of IgA located within the mesangium, where they most likely activate complement through the alternative pathway. The diagnostic immunofluorescence finding is intense mesangial staining for IgA (more intense for IgA than IgG or IgM), almost always accompanied by C3. Granular capillary membrane deposition (choice A) is seen in membranous nephropathy and postinfectious GN. Linear basement membrane staining (choice B) is the pattern of anti-GBM disease (Goodpasture syndrome). Perivascular location (choice D) is not a feature of IgA nephropathy. Subepithelial deposits (choice E) characterize membranous nephropathy.
keypoints: ⭐ IgA nephropathy IF: mesangial IgA deposits (dominant) + C3; IgA > IgG, IgM — diagnostic pattern
EM confirms mesangial electron-dense deposits
Mesangial IgA activates alternative complement pathway → mesangial cell proliferation → hematuria
Henoch-Schönlein purpura (HSP/IgAV) has identical renal pathology but also involves skin, joints, and GI tract

questions.stem: A 25-year-old man complains of intermittent hematuria over the past 8 years. Urinalysis shows microscopic hematuria. Urine cultures are negative. A renal biopsy displays mesangial proliferation within some glomeruli, whereas others appear normal. Immunofluorescence staining discloses mesangial deposition of IgA. Which of the following is the appropriate pathologic diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Focal proliferative glomerulonephritis is characterized by pathologic changes in some (focal) glomeruli, while others appear normal. This category includes IgA nephropathy (Berger disease), which presents with mesangial IgA deposits and mesangial cell proliferation in some glomeruli. The clinical presentation is variable, reflecting the varied pathologic severity. Amyloid nephropathy (choice A) shows amorphous deposits without proliferation. Crescentic GN (choice B) involves >50% of glomeruli with crescents. Membranous nephropathy (choice D) features subepithelial deposits without proliferation. Nodular diabetic glomerulosclerosis (choice E) shows diffuse and nodular mesangial matrix expansion without IgA.
keypoints: ⭐ Focal proliferative GN = changes in SOME glomeruli; includes IgA nephropathy, HSP, focal lupus nephritis (Class III)
IgA nephropathy: mesangial IgA + mesangial cell proliferation in affected glomeruli, normal in others = focal distribution
Diffuse proliferative GN = ALL glomeruli involved (e.g., poststreptococcal GN, Class IV lupus)
Pathologic diagnosis 'focal' ≠ benign — IgA nephropathy can progress to ESRD
questions.imageAlt: Renal biopsy showing mesangial hypercellularity in some glomeruli with others appearing normal
questions.imageCaption: PAS-stained biopsy showing mesangial cell proliferation in some glomeruli — focal proliferative GN (IgA nephropathy)

questions.stem: An 8-year-old boy presents with headaches, dizziness, and malaise approximately 2 weeks after a severe sore throat. His mother describes puffiness of his face and darkening of his urine. On physical examination, there is anasarca, hypertension (190/130 mm Hg), and tachycardia. The urine is scanty and brownish red. Urinalysis shows 3+ proteinuria. Microscopic examination of the urine discloses numerous RBCs, as well as occasional granular and red cell casts. A renal biopsy is stained by direct immunofluorescence for complement C3. Which of the following best describes the pattern of immunofluorescence observed in this renal biopsy?
choices: Dense deposits in glomerular crescents between epithelial cells
Granular deposits along the perimesangial reflections
Linear staining along the glomerular basement membranes
questions.explanation: The most distinctive ultrastructural and immunofluorescence features of acute postinfectious glomerulonephritis are subepithelial dense deposits shaped like 'humps' (on EM), invariably accompanied by mesangial and subendothelial deposits. On immunofluorescence for C3, the pattern shows a combination of subepithelial and subendothelial deposits ('lumpy-bumpy' granular pattern). These mesangial and subendothelial deposits are probably more important in pathogenesis due to their proximity to inflammatory mediator systems in the blood. Linear staining along the GBM (choice D) is the anti-GBM pattern of Goodpasture syndrome. Deposits limited to the mesangium (choice B) are characteristic of IgA nephropathy. Dense deposits in crescents (choice A) are seen in anti-GBM disease. Perimesangial deposits alone (choice C) would suggest early IgA nephropathy.
keypoints: ⭐ Poststreptococcal GN IF: granular 'lumpy-bumpy' pattern of C3 and IgG — subepithelial + subendothelial + mesangial deposits
EM: subepithelial 'humps' = most distinctive finding of postinfectious GN
Compare: Linear IF = anti-GBM (Goodpasture); Mesangial = IgA nephropathy; Granular capillary loop = membranous GN
Hypocomplementemia (↓C3) in >90% of cases; C3 normalizes in 6-8 weeks — key distinguishing feature from MPGN
questions.imageAlt: Direct immunofluorescence for C3 showing granular lumpy-bumpy deposits in a glomerulus
questions.imageCaption: Direct IF for C3: granular 'lumpy-bumpy' subepithelial and subendothelial deposits — acute poststreptococcal GN

questions.stem: Which of the following is the most likely cause of acute postinfectious glomerulonephritis in the patient described in Question 23 (8-year-old boy with nephritic syndrome 2 weeks after sore throat)?
questions.explanation: Acute postinfectious glomerulonephritis is an immune complex disease most commonly occurring after infection with group A (β-hemolytic) streptococci. The primary infection involves the pharynx (in temperate climates) or the skin in impetigo (in hot and humid environments). Only certain nephritogenic strains cause GN. Immune complexes form with bacterial antigens and deposit in glomeruli. Occasional cases are caused by staphylococcal infection (choice E) — notably the proportion of staphylococcal cases has been increasing in recent years. Rare cases result from viral (e.g., hepatitis B) or parasitic (e.g., malaria) infections. E. coli (choice A) causes UTI and HUS, not postinfectious GN. Epstein-Barr virus (choice B) and Klebsiella (choice D) do not typically cause GN.
keypoints: ⭐ Group A β-hemolytic streptococci = most common cause of acute postinfectious GN; only NEPHRITOGENIC strains cause GN (not same strains as rheumatic fever)
Pharyngitis → GN (2-week latency); impetigo/skin → GN (4-6 week latency)
ASO titer elevated after pharyngitis; anti-DNase B elevated after skin infection
Staphylococcal GN is increasingly recognized, especially in elderly patients with indwelling devices

questions.stem: Which of the following is the most likely outcome of glomerulonephritis in the patient described in Question 23 (8-year-old boy with acute poststreptococcal glomerulonephritis)?
questions.explanation: Overt nephritis after postinfectious glomerulonephritis in children usually resolves after several weeks, although hematuria and especially proteinuria may persist for several months. The vast majority of children with poststreptococcal GN recover completely without progression to chronic renal disease. This distinguishes it from adult-onset cases, where the prognosis is somewhat less favorable. Bilateral cortical necrosis (choice A) is caused by hypovolemic shock, not GN. Nephrotic syndrome (choice B) is not an expected transition from poststreptococcal GN. MPGN (choice C) is a separate entity. Crescentic GN (choice E) is a rare, aggressive complication seen in a minority of postinfectious GN cases.
keypoints: ⭐ Poststreptococcal GN in children: excellent prognosis — complete recovery in >95% of cases
Hematuria and proteinuria may persist for months before resolution
Adult poststreptococcal GN: less favorable — ~20% develop chronic renal insufficiency
Prescribe antibiotics (penicillin) to eradicate strep and prevent spread, but antibiotics do NOT change GN course once started

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman with systemic lupus erythematosus presents with oliguria. Laboratory studies show elevated serum levels of creatinine and BUN. Urinalysis reveals 4+ proteinuria and hematuria. The renal biopsy exhibits segmental endocapillary hypercellularity and thickening of capillary walls, and 90% of the glomeruli appear hypercellular. Which of the following is the appropriate pathologic diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Systemic lupus erythematosus is an autoimmune disease characterized by B-cell hyperactivity and production of autoantibodies. Diffuse proliferative glomerulonephritis (WHO/ISN Class IV) is the most severe form of lupus nephritis, with widespread involvement of glomeruli (here, 90%), diffuse proliferation of mesangial and endothelial cells, and immune complex deposits visible on all sides of the basement membrane. The thickened capillary walls containing subendothelial deposits are known as 'wire-loop' lesions. Immune complex deposits are present on both sides of the basement membrane, in the mesangial areas, and inside capillary loops. Membranous nephropathy (choice D) may occur in SLE (Class V) but does not show hypercellularity. MPGN type II (choice C) is unrelated to SLE. The biopsy shows hypercellularity, ruling out FSGS (choice B).
keypoints: ⭐ Diffuse proliferative GN (Class IV lupus): worst prognosis; >50% glomeruli involved; 'wire-loop' lesions = subendothelial immune complex deposits → thickened capillary walls
Lupus nephritis classifications: I=minimal, II=mesangial, III=focal (<50% glomeruli), IV=diffuse (>50% glomeruli), V=membranous
Class IV: hypocomplementemia (↓C3, C4), elevated anti-dsDNA; treatment = mycophenolate + steroids
SLE nephritis 'full house' IF: IgA, IgG, IgM, C3, C1q all positive
questions.imageAlt: Renal biopsy showing diffuse endocapillary hypercellularity with wire-loop lesions and thickened capillary walls
questions.imageCaption: H&E: diffuse proliferative glomerulonephritis with 'wire-loop' lesions — Class IV lupus nephritis (SLE)

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man with a history of smoking presents with hematuria and bloody sputum. Over the next 2 days, he develops oliguria and renal failure, after which he is placed on dialysis. A renal biopsy is stained with fluorescein-conjugated goat antihuman IgG. Which of the following best describes the pattern of direct immunofluorescence observed on this photomicrograph?
choices: Finely granular along the perimesangial reflections
Linear along the glomerular basement membrane
questions.explanation: This presentation — pulmonary hemorrhage (hemoptysis) plus rapidly progressive renal failure — is classic for Goodpasture syndrome (anti-GBM antibody disease). The disease is mediated by autoantibodies against the noncollagenous domain of type IV collagen, which is present in both the GBM and pulmonary alveolar basement membrane. The characteristic immunofluorescence finding is diffuse linear staining of GBMs for IgG, reflecting autoantibodies uniformly bound along the entire basement membrane — in contrast to the granular ('lumpy-bumpy') pattern of immune complex deposition. Granular peripheral humps (choice E) are seen in poststreptococcal GN. Mesangial staining (choice D) indicates IgA nephropathy. Perimesangial granular staining (choice B) is seen in early immune complex deposition.
keypoints: ⭐ Goodpasture syndrome: anti-GBM antibodies (anti-type IV collagen NC1 domain) → LINEAR IgG IF along GBM — diagnostic pattern
Clinical triad: hemoptysis + hematuria + rapidly progressive renal failure
Linear IF = anti-GBM; Granular IF = immune complex; Pauci-immune (no IF) = ANCA-associated
Treatment: plasmapheresis (remove anti-GBM antibodies) + steroids + cyclophosphamide
questions.imageAlt: Direct immunofluorescence for IgG showing linear staining along the glomerular basement membrane
questions.imageCaption: Direct IF (IgG): linear GBM staining — anti-GBM antibody disease (Goodpasture syndrome)

questions.stem: A 54-year-old woman with squamous cell carcinoma of the lung develops bilateral pitting edema of the lower extremities. Laboratory studies show hyperlipidemia, hypoalbuminemia, and 4+ proteinuria. Urinalysis reveals no inflammatory cells or RBCs. Renal biopsy in this patient would most likely show which of the following patterns of glomerulopathy?
questions.explanation: Many malignant neoplasms may be accompanied by paraneoplastic syndromes, among which is membranous nephropathy. This is a common paraneoplastic renal complication of solid tumors, especially carcinomas of the lung, colon, stomach, and breast. Immune complex deposition in the subepithelial zone produces nephrotic syndrome (heavy proteinuria, hypoalbuminemia, hyperlipidemia, edema) without hematuria. Other causes of secondary membranous nephropathy include SLE, hepatitis B, and drugs (penicillamine). IgA nephropathy (choice A) and Goodpasture syndrome (choice B) are not paraneoplastic. Minimal change glomerulopathy (choice D) is usually found in children and is not a paraneoplastic disorder. Nodular glomerulosclerosis (choice E) reflects diabetic lesions.
keypoints: ⭐ Paraneoplastic nephrotic syndrome: membranous nephropathy is the most common glomerular lesion associated with solid tumors
Malignancy-associated membranous GN: lung, colon, stomach, breast carcinomas — workup for occult malignancy in adults with new membranous GN
Minimal change disease can be associated with Hodgkin lymphoma (not solid tumors)
Secondary causes of membranous GN: malignancy, SLE, HBV, drugs (penicillamine, gold, NSAIDs, captopril)

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man with a history of smoking suddenly develops oliguria, hematuria, and hemoptysis. Serologic studies reveal antibodies to the glomerular basement membrane (GBM). A renal biopsy is shown. Which of the following pathologic changes is visible by light microscopy in this biopsy specimen?
questions.explanation: Crescentic glomerulonephritis is the morphologic equivalent of rapidly progressive renal failure in Goodpasture syndrome. Anti-GBM antibodies attack the type IV collagen in the GBM, causing rupture of the GBM and extravasation of blood, fibrin, and inflammatory cells into the urinary space (between Bowman's capsule and the glomerular capillary tufts). This leads to the formation of hypercellular, crescent-like structures composed of proliferating parietal epithelial cells, visceral epithelial cells, and macrophages. Over 90% of patients with anti-GBM disease have glomerular crescents by light microscopy. The crescents fill and obliterate the urinary space. The other choices are not representative of the crescent pattern of Goodpasture syndrome.
keypoints: ⭐ Goodpasture syndrome light microscopy: CRESCENTS in urinary space (= RPGN morphology)
Crescents = parietal epithelial cells + macrophages proliferating in Bowman's space after GBM rupture
>50% of glomeruli with crescents = rapidly progressive GN; prognosis poor without urgent treatment
Goodpasture: anti-GBM (linear IF) → crescentic GN + pulmonary hemorrhage; RPGN Type I
questions.imageAlt: Renal biopsy showing crescents in the urinary space compressing the glomerular tuft
questions.imageCaption: H&E: crescentic glomerulonephritis — cellular crescents filling Bowman's space (Goodpasture syndrome, RPGN)

questions.stem: A 52-year-old woman who suffers from diabetes mellitus and frequent urinary tract infections presents with a 3-day history of flank pain, undulating fever, and general malaise. A CBC shows neutrophilic leukocytosis (16,000/μL). Urine cultures reveal more than 100,000 bacterial colonies, composed predominantly of Gram-negative microorganisms. Blood pressure is 170/100 mm Hg, BUN is 30 mg/dL, and creatinine is 2.0 mg/dL. Urinalysis shows 2+ sugar and 1+ protein. Microscopic examination of the urine sediment reveals neutrophils and occasional leukocyte casts. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The combination of fever, flank pain, leukocytosis, positive urine culture with >100,000 Gram-negative bacterial colonies, glucosuria (diabetes predisposes via rich growth medium), leukocyte casts, and neutrophils in the urine indicates acute pyelonephritis. Gram-negative bacteria (most commonly E. coli) cause 80% of cases; they reach the kidney via ascending infection from the lower urinary tract. Leukocyte casts confirm upper urinary tract (renal parenchymal) infection, distinguishing pyelonephritis from cystitis. Diabetic nephropathy (choice C) causes proteinuria but not acute systemic illness with bacterial casts. ATN (choice B) does not produce WBC casts or fever. Postinfectious GN (choice D) follows streptococcal infection and presents with hematuria, not pyuria. Nephrolithiasis (choice E) causes colicky pain without leukocyte casts.
keypoints: ⭐ Acute pyelonephritis: fever + flank pain + WBC casts + bacteriuria >100,000 colonies/mL + Gram-negative bacteria (E. coli 80%)
WBC casts = upper UTI (pyelonephritis) vs. just pyuria = can be lower UTI
Diabetics are at increased risk due to glucosuria (bacterial growth medium) and impaired neutrophil function
Treatment: fluoroquinolones or trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole; IV ceftriaxone for severe/hospitalized cases

questions.stem: A 22-year-old woman in the second trimester of pregnancy presents with flank pain, fever of 38.7°C (103°F), and chills. Hemoglobin is 13.4 g/dL, WBCs are elevated (13,500/μL with 78% neutrophils), and there are 265,000 platelets/μL. Physical examination reveals costovertebral angle tenderness. The urine shows numerous WBCs and WBC casts. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: This pregnant woman presents with the classic features of acute pyelonephritis: fever, chills, flank pain, costovertebral angle tenderness, leukocytosis, and — critically — WBC casts in the urine. WBC casts indicate upper urinary tract infection (renal parenchymal involvement), distinguishing pyelonephritis from lower UTI. Pregnancy increases the risk of pyelonephritis due to urinary stasis from uterine compression of ureters and progesterone-induced ureteral smooth muscle relaxation. Cystitis (choice A) and urethritis (choice E) are lower UTI conditions that do not produce leukocyte casts. Endometritis (choice B) presents with uterine tenderness and vaginal discharge, not urinary casts. Glomerulonephritis (choice C) produces RBC casts, not WBC casts, and features proteinuria/hematuria rather than systemic infection signs.
keypoints: ⭐ Pyelonephritis vs. cystitis: pyelonephritis = WBC casts + fever + CVA tenderness; cystitis = dysuria + frequency, NO casts, NO systemic signs
Pregnancy increases UTI/pyelonephritis risk: ureteral compression + progesterone → urinary stasis → bacterial ascent
Asymptomatic bacteriuria in pregnancy must be treated (→ pyelonephritis if untreated, premature labor)
Treat pregnancy UTI with nitrofurantoin (avoid in third trimester), cephalosporins; avoid fluoroquinolones

questions.stem: Which of the following is the most likely cause of the renal disease in the patient described in Question 31 (22-year-old pregnant woman with acute pyelonephritis)?
questions.explanation: Acute pyelonephritis and chronic pyelonephritis are bacterial diseases that usually develop from ascending infections. Gram-negative bacteria from the feces — most commonly E. coli — cause 80% of cases. The pathogenesis depends on bacterial infection of the urine, reflux of infected urine up the ureters, and entry of bacteria through the papillae into the renal parenchyma. Bladder infections, which more commonly affect females due to a shorter urethra, lack of antibacterial prostatic secretions, and facilitation of bacterial migration by sexual intercourse, often precede acute pyelonephritis. Gram-positive bacteria (choice B) can cause pyelonephritis but are not the most common cause. Viruses and mycobacteria (choices C, E) do not ordinarily cause acute pyelonephritis. Immune complex deposition (choice D) is associated with glomerular, not tubulointerstitial, disease.
keypoints: ⭐ Pyelonephritis: Gram-negative bacteria (E. coli 80%) via ascending route; E. coli virulence factors include type 1 and P fimbriae for uroepithelial adhesion
Risk factors: female sex (short urethra), sexual intercourse, pregnancy, urinary obstruction, DM, vesicoureteral reflux
Other Gram-negatives: Klebsiella, Proteus, Enterobacter; Proteus produces urease → alkaline urine → struvite stones
Gram-positive causes: Staphylococcus saprophyticus (#2 in young women), Enterococcus faecalis

questions.stem: A 50-year-old woman complains of severe headaches and dizziness. The patient has a history of repeated urinary tract infections. The blood pressure is 180/110 mm Hg. Laboratory studies show elevated levels of BUN (38 mg/dL) and creatinine (2.8 mg/dL). A CT scan of the lower abdomen reveals small, irregularly shaped kidneys with deep coarse scars. A percutaneous renal biopsy shows tubular dilation and atrophy, interstitial scarring, and many tubules with eosinophilic hyaline casts resembling thyroid follicle colloid. Which of the following is the appropriate diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Chronic pyelonephritis is caused by recurrent and persistent bacterial infections secondary to urinary tract obstruction, urine reflux, or both. Patients experience episodic fever, flank pain, and recurrent UTIs. The biopsy shows the characteristic 'thyroidization' of the kidney — tubular dilation and atrophy with eosinophilic hyaline casts that resemble colloid of thyroid follicles — accompanied by interstitial scarring and chronic inflammatory infiltrate. Imaging reveals small, irregularly shaped kidneys with deep coarse scars (especially over dilated calyces). Acute pyelonephritis (choice A) does not produce scarred, shrunken kidneys. ATN (choice B) is not related to bacterial infections. Nephrosclerosis (choice D) is caused by hypertension and produces uniformly granular small kidneys, not coarse scars. Tubulointerstitial nephritis (choice E) lacks the bacterial infection history.
keypoints: ⭐ Chronic pyelonephritis: coarse, irregular cortical scars overlying dilated calyces; 'thyroidization' = tubules filled with eosinophilic hyaline casts resembling thyroid colloid
Caused by repeated bacterial infections + VUR (vesicoureteral reflux) or obstruction
Distinguish from nephrosclerosis: nephrosclerosis → uniformly granular small kidneys; chronic PNA → coarse, irregular scars
Complications: secondary hypertension, progressive CKD/ESRD
questions.imageAlt: Renal biopsy showing tubular atrophy and dilation with eosinophilic hyaline casts resembling thyroid colloid (thyroidization)
questions.imageCaption: H&E: 'thyroidization' of the kidney — dilated tubules with colloid-like casts and interstitial fibrosis; chronic pyelonephritis

questions.stem: The pathogenesis of the renal disease in the patient described in Question 33 (50-year-old woman with chronic pyelonephritis) is related to which of the following conditions?
questions.explanation: Chronic pyelonephritis is caused by recurrent and persistent bacterial infection secondary to urinary tract obstruction, urine reflux, or both. Each episode of acute pyelonephritis causes inflammation and scarring of the renal parenchyma, and repeated bouts lead to progressive cortical scarring, tubular atrophy, and calyceal deformation. Amyloidosis (choice A), anti-GBM disease (choice B), and chronic hepatitis B (choice C) all cause glomerular disease, not tubulointerstitial scarring from bacterial infection. Hypertension (choice D) is a vascular disorder that causes uniformly granular small kidneys (nephrosclerosis) without the deep cortical scars of chronic pyelonephritis, though hypertension can complicate chronic pyelonephritis.
keypoints: ⭐ Chronic pyelonephritis pathogenesis: repeated acute pyelonephritis → progressive renal scarring; requires underlying predisposing factor (VUR, obstruction)
Vesicoureteral reflux (VUR) = most important predisposing factor in children; reflux nephropathy
Obstructive uropathy (BPH, stones, strictures) = most important in adults
Each acute pyelonephritis episode scars the medulla and cortex → progressive CKD over years

questions.stem: A 52-year-old woman presents with swelling of her ankles of 6 weeks in duration. Physical examination reveals an obese woman (BMI = 32 kg/m²) with pitting edema of the lower extremities and periorbital edema. Laboratory studies show hyperlipidemia and hypoalbuminemia. Urinalysis discloses 3+ proteinuria and 3+ glucosuria but no evidence of inflammatory cells or RBCs. A kidney biopsy stained with PAS displays a prominent increase in the mesangial matrix forming nodular lesions, and thickening of capillary basement membranes. Which of the following is the most likely pathologic diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Diabetic glomerulosclerosis is the most common cause of end-stage renal disease in the United States, accounting for one third of all patients with chronic renal failure. The combination of glucosuria, proteinuria, hyperlipidemia, hypoalbuminemia, and nephrotic-range proteinuria in an obese patient points to diabetes mellitus with diabetic nephropathy. The PAS-positive (glycoprotein-rich) nodular lesions — Kimmelstiel-Wilson nodules — represent pathognomonic nodular diabetic glomerulosclerosis with mesangial matrix expansion, capillary basement membrane thickening, and hyalinized arterioles. Amyloid nephropathy (choice B) also produces nodules, but is Congo red-positive (not PAS-positive). Membranoproliferative GN (choice E) shows mesangial proliferation and double-contour GBM, not nodular sclerosis. MPGN and acute GN do not produce glucosuria.
keypoints: ⭐ Diabetic nephropathy: Kimmelstiel-Wilson nodules = PAS-positive nodular mesangial matrix expansion + GBM thickening + hyaline arteriosclerosis
Progression: microalbuminuria → overt proteinuria → nephrotic syndrome → CKD/ESRD over 10-20 years
Glucosuria in urinalysis + nephrotic syndrome = think diabetic nephropathy first
Distinguish from amyloid: Congo red (+) with apple-green birefringence = amyloid; PAS (+), Congo red (−) = diabetic nodules
questions.imageAlt: PAS-stained renal biopsy showing nodular mesangial matrix expansion (Kimmelstiel-Wilson nodules) and GBM thickening
questions.imageCaption: PAS stain: Kimmelstiel-Wilson nodules — nodular diabetic glomerulosclerosis with mesangial matrix expansion

questions.stem: Which of the following serum abnormalities is expected in the patient described in Question 35 (52-year-old woman with diabetic glomerulosclerosis)?
questions.explanation: The cardinal biochemical manifestation of diabetes mellitus is hyperglycemia. In diabetic nephropathy, abnormal non-enzymatic glycosylation of serum and matrix proteins — including those of the glomerular basement membrane and mesangial matrix — induces binding of plasma proteins and stimulates excessive matrix production. As a result, GBMs are thickened and hyperpermeable to albumin, leading to proteinuria. Overt proteinuria occurs 10 to 15 years after the onset of diabetes and may progress to nephrotic syndrome. Hyperbilirubinemia (choice A) indicates liver disease. Hypergammaglobulinemia (choice B) suggests chronic inflammation or plasma cell dyscrasia. Hyperuricemia (choice D) indicates gout or high cell turnover. Hypobilirubinemia (choice E) is not a recognized clinical entity.
keypoints: ⭐ Diabetes mellitus → hyperglycemia → non-enzymatic glycosylation of GBM and mesangial proteins → GBM thickening → proteinuria → nephrotic syndrome
Mechanism of diabetic nephropathy: hyperglycemia → advanced glycation end products (AGEs) → mesangial expansion + GBM thickening + afferent/efferent arteriolar hyalinosis
Hyperfiltration (early) → microalbuminuria → macroalbuminuria → nephrotic syndrome → GFR decline
ACE inhibitors/ARBs are renoprotective in diabetic nephropathy regardless of blood pressure

questions.stem: A 40-year-old man with Alport syndrome presents with a 3-month history of headaches. His blood pressure is 165/100 mm Hg. A urinalysis shows 3+ proteinuria and 2+ hematuria. Laboratory studies disclose elevated levels of BUN (48 mg/dL) and creatinine (3.6 mg/dL). This patient's renal disease is caused by mutation in a gene that encodes which of the following extracellular matrix proteins?
questions.explanation: Hereditary nephritis (Alport syndrome) is caused by mutations in genes encoding type IV collagen chains (most commonly COL4A5, X-linked; or COL4A3/COL4A4, autosomal recessive). Type IV collagen is the major structural component of the glomerular basement membrane. Abnormal type IV collagen leads to a structurally abnormal GBM that allows hematuria early in life and progressive proteinuria, renal failure, and hypertension later. Alport syndrome is also associated with sensorineural hearing loss and ocular abnormalities (anterior lenticonus). Fibrillin (choice C) is mutated in Marfan syndrome. Entactin (choice B), fibronectin (choice D), and laminin (choice E) are components of the extracellular matrix but are not the proteins mutated in Alport syndrome.
keypoints: ⭐ Alport syndrome: mutations in type IV COLLAGEN (COL4A3, COL4A4 [AR] or COL4A5 [X-linked]) → abnormal GBM → progressive nephritis
Classic triad: progressive nephritis (hematuria → proteinuria → ESRD) + sensorineural hearing loss + ocular defects (anterior lenticonus)
X-linked most common (80%): males affected severely; females are carriers with variable hematuria
EM: thinning, thickening, and splitting (lamellation) of GBM — pathognomonic 'basket-weave' appearance

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man presents with fever and rash after beginning treatment with penicillin 2 weeks earlier for a sinus infection. Urinalysis shows 3+ hematuria, as well as mononuclear cells, neutrophils, and eosinophils. A percutaneous renal biopsy shows infiltrates of activated lymphocytes (T lymphocytes) and admixed eosinophils in the interstitium. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Drug-induced (hypersensitivity) acute tubulointerstitial nephritis reflects a cell-mediated (type IV) immune response. It is characterized histologically by interstitial infiltrates of activated T lymphocytes and admixed eosinophils — a pattern indicating a type IV hypersensitivity reaction. The classic clinical presentation is development of renal failure, fever, rash, and eosinophiluria approximately 2 weeks after starting a drug (classic offenders: penicillin, NSAIDs, PPIs, sulfonamides, diuretics). Most patients recover fully if the drug is discontinued. The other choices are not associated with eosinophilic interstitial infiltrates and are not related to drug hypersensitivity. Glomerulonephritis (choices C, D) involves glomerular pathology, not interstitial inflammation.
keypoints: ⭐ Drug-induced acute tubulointerstitial nephritis: fever + rash + eosinophilia + hematuria ~2 weeks after drug → interstitial T-cell and eosinophil infiltrate
Classic drugs: penicillins/cephalosporins, NSAIDs, PPIs, sulfonamides, rifampin, thiazides
Eosinophiluria (Hansel stain) supports diagnosis but is not always present
Treatment: stop offending drug; steroids if no improvement in 1-2 weeks
questions.imageAlt: Renal biopsy showing interstitial infiltrate of T lymphocytes and eosinophils with relative glomerular sparing
questions.imageCaption: H&E: interstitial T-cell and eosinophil infiltrate — drug-induced (penicillin) acute tubulointerstitial nephritis

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man with a history of coronary artery disease, peripheral vascular disease, and a recent heart attack suddenly develops painless hematuria. He subsequently suffers a massive stroke and expires. The patient's kidney at autopsy shows a peripheral infarct with marked pallor extending to the subcapsular surface. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Renal cortical infarcts are caused primarily by arterial obstruction — most commonly embolization to the interlobar or larger branches of the renal artery. Common sources of emboli include mural thrombi (from myocardial infarction, as in this patient with a recent MI), infected cardiac valves, and complicated atherosclerotic plaques. The infarct appears as a peripheral wedge-shaped area of pallor extending to the subcapsular surface. This patient's history of CAD, PVD, and recent MI provides the embolic source. Benign and malignant nephrosclerosis (choices A, E) are vascular disorders that are diffuse rather than localized. Cortical abscess (choice C) would show purulent material and fever. Chronic pyelonephritis (choice B) causes irregular cortical scars over dilated calyces, not pale wedge-shaped infarcts.
keypoints: ⭐ Renal cortical infarct: embolic occlusion of renal artery branch → wedge-shaped pale infarct extending to renal surface
Common embolic sources: mural thrombus post-MI, bacterial endocarditis (infected emboli), aortic atherosclerosis
Presentation: sudden flank pain + hematuria ± hypertension; painless hematuria in this case
Distinguish from abscess (purulent, infected) and pyelonephritis (cortical scars, not pale infarcts)
questions.imageAlt: Gross specimen of kidney showing a pale wedge-shaped peripheral cortical infarct extending to the subcapsular surface
questions.imageCaption: Gross kidney specimen: pale wedge-shaped cortical infarct — embolic renal artery occlusion (post-MI)

questions.stem: A 36-year-old woman in the third trimester of pregnancy (gravida II, para I) presents to the emergency room with the sudden onset of severe vaginal bleeding. Ultrasound examination of the abdomen discloses abruptio placentae. A healthy neonate is delivered; however, the mother's blood loss is uncontrollable. She becomes hypotensive and obtunded and subsequently dies of hypovolemic shock. Autopsy of the kidneys reveals massive cortical pallor bilaterally. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Bilateral renal cortical necrosis is a syndrome characterized by massive ischemic necrosis involving large portions of the cortex of both kidneys. It typically occurs in the setting of hypovolemia and endotoxic shock, and is a classic catastrophic complication of obstetric emergencies such as abruptio placentae, septic abortion, or eclampsia. The combination of massive hemorrhage, hypotension, and disseminated intravascular coagulation leads to diffuse thrombosis of glomerular and interlobular arteries, causing ischemic necrosis. Histologically, cortical necrosis is more widespread than a focal infarct. The other choices are not associated with grossly visible bilateral cortical necrosis in the setting of obstetric hemorrhage.
keypoints: ⭐ Bilateral renal cortical necrosis: obstetric emergency (abruptio placentae, septic abortion) + hypovolemic shock + DIC → bilateral cortical ischemia/necrosis
Mechanism: DIC → glomerular and interlobular artery thrombosis → cortical ischemia (medulla spared via vasa recta blood supply)
Grossly: pale/white cortex bilaterally at autopsy
Prognosis: severe AKI; dialysis required; poor recovery of function
questions.imageAlt: Gross autopsy specimen of kidney showing diffuse bilateral cortical pallor/necrosis
questions.imageCaption: Gross autopsy: bilateral cortical pallor — bilateral renal cortical necrosis (abruptio placentae, hypovolemic shock)

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man undergoes resection of an abdominal aneurysm, which is complicated by massive hemorrhage. Two days after surgery, the patient develops acute renal insufficiency. He is placed on dialysis but suffers a massive heart attack and dies. Microscopic examination of the kidneys at autopsy reveals necrotic epithelial cells within the lumina of some tubules, with enlarged regenerative epithelial cells also visible. What is the appropriate diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Acute tubular necrosis (ATN) is a severe but potentially reversible impairment of tubular epithelial function caused by ischemia or toxic injury, resulting in acute renal failure. Ischemic ATN results from reduced renal perfusion — here from massive intraoperative hemorrhage. Tubular epithelial cells, with their high metabolic activity, are particularly sensitive to hypoxia. The classic histologic findings include focal denudation of the tubular basement membrane, necrotic tubular epithelial cells sloughing into the lumina, and enlarged regenerative epithelial cells indicating attempted repair. Glomeruli and blood vessels appear normal. Acute interstitial nephritis (choice A) and eosinophilic interstitial nephritis (choice C) feature interstitial inflammation, not isolated tubular necrosis. Fanconi syndrome (choice D) is a chronic proximal tubular dysfunction. Polyarteritis nodosa (choice E) causes vascular, not tubular, injury.
keypoints: ⭐ Ischemic ATN: necrotic tubular epithelial cells in lumen + enlarged regenerative cells + tubular basement membrane denudation; glomeruli normal
Causes: ischemia (shock, sepsis, surgery) vs. nephrotoxic (aminoglycosides, contrast, cisplatin, myoglobinuria)
Muddy brown granular casts in urine = ATN (tubular epithelial cell casts)
ATN phases: oliguric phase (1-2 weeks) → polyuric phase → recovery; potentially fully reversible with supportive care
questions.imageAlt: Renal biopsy showing necrotic tubular epithelial cells within tubular lumina and enlarged regenerative epithelial cells
questions.imageCaption: H&E: necrotic tubular cells in lumen with regenerative changes — ischemic acute tubular necrosis (post-hemorrhagic)

questions.stem: A 33-year-old woman in her third trimester of pregnancy (gravida I, para 0) is rushed to the emergency room after suffering a seizure. The patient is hypertensive and laboratory studies show that the patient manifests nephritic syndrome. What is the appropriate diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Preeclampsia is characterized by the triad of hypertension, proteinuria (nephritic syndrome features), and edema complicating the third trimester of pregnancy. When these features are accompanied by grand mal seizures, the condition is called eclampsia. The glomeruli show characteristic 'endotheliosis' — uniform glomerular enlargement with swollen endothelial cells creating an apparently bloodless glomerular tuft. Preeclampsia alone (choice E) is the diagnosis without seizures. ATN (choice A) and crescentic GN (choice B) are not ordinarily complications of pregnancy. Malignant nephrosclerosis (choice D) is associated with severely elevated BP and 'onion-skinning' of renal arteries, not the obstetric context here.
keypoints: ⭐ Preeclampsia = HTN + proteinuria + edema in pregnancy (>20 weeks); Eclampsia = preeclampsia + SEIZURES
Renal finding in preeclampsia: glomerular endotheliosis (swollen endothelial cells → bloodless glomeruli)
Treatment: magnesium sulfate for seizure prophylaxis/treatment; antihypertensives; definitive = delivery
Risk factors: nulliparity, multiple gestation, prior preeclampsia, DM, CKD, antiphospholipid syndrome

questions.stem: A 70-year-old obese woman (BMI = 34 kg/m²) presents with a 3-month history of progressive renal insufficiency. She has a longstanding history of hypertension. An intravenous pyelogram shows that both kidneys are small, and the pelves and calyces appear normal. Examination of the kidneys at autopsy reveals symmetrically shrunken small kidneys, with a uniformly finely granular surface. Which of the following is the appropriate diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Hypertensive nephrosclerosis (benign nephrosclerosis) leads to progressive obliteration of glomeruli and may cause end-stage kidney disease. It is identified in approximately 15% of patients with even mild-to-moderate hypertension. The gross appearance of uniformly, symmetrically shrunken small kidneys with a finely granular surface (from cortical scarring as individual glomeruli are lost) is the hallmark. Histologically, most glomeruli are hyalinized, tubules are atrophic or replaced by fibrous tissue, and arterioles show hyaline arteriolosclerosis (concentric hyaline thickening). Amyloidosis (choice A) enlarges the kidneys. Hydronephrosis (choice B) dilates the collecting system. Ischemic ATN (choice C) is acute. Tubulointerstitial nephritis (choice E) is not specifically related to hypertension and does not produce uniformly granular kidneys.
keypoints: ⭐ Hypertensive nephrosclerosis (benign): symmetrically small kidneys + uniformly finely granular surface + hyaline arteriolosclerosis on histology
Contrast with chronic pyelonephritis: coarse, IRREGULAR scars over dilated calyces vs. nephrosclerosis: fine, UNIFORM granularity
Hyaline arteriolosclerosis = PAS-positive hyaline material in arteriolar walls replacing smooth muscle cells
Malignant nephrosclerosis: severely elevated BP + 'onion-skin' hyperplastic arteriolosclerosis + fibrinoid necrosis
questions.imageAlt: Gross kidney specimen showing symmetrically shrunken small kidneys with a uniformly finely granular cortical surface
questions.imageCaption: Gross specimen: symmetrically small kidneys with uniformly granular surface — hypertensive nephrosclerosis (benign)

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man presents with acute renal insufficiency. He treated his garden last week with a number of herbicides and insecticides, some of which may have contained heavy metals. Laboratory studies confirm oliguria and increased levels of BUN (54 mg/dL) and creatinine (3.7 mg/dL). A renal biopsy shows widespread necrosis of proximal tubular epithelial cells with sparing of distal and collecting tubules. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Nephrotoxic ATN is caused by chemically induced injury to tubular epithelial cells. Because proximal tubular cells absorb and concentrate chemicals from the glomerular filtrate, they are the preferred targets for certain toxins including heavy metals (mercury, lead, arsenic), aminoglycosides, radiographic contrast agents, and organic solvents. The biopsy characteristically shows widespread necrosis of proximal tubular epithelial cells with relative sparing of distal tubules and collecting ducts — reflecting the segment-specific concentration of nephrotoxins. Tubulointerstitial nephritis (choice E) may result from certain drug exposures but features prominent interstitial inflammation with lymphocytes and eosinophils, not isolated tubular necrosis. Bilateral cortical necrosis (choice B) involves diffuse cortical ischemia. RPGN (choice D) involves glomerular pathology. Papillary necrosis (choice C) affects renal papillary tips.
keypoints: ⭐ Nephrotoxic ATN: preferential proximal tubular necrosis (concentration of toxins here); distal tubules spared
Classic nephrotoxins: aminoglycosides, cisplatin, heavy metals (mercury, lead), radiocontrast, myoglobin (rhabdomyolysis), ethylene glycol
Contrast with ischemic ATN: ischemic = focal tubular necrosis + basement membrane rupture throughout nephron
Fractional excretion of Na (FENa) >2% in ATN; <1% in prerenal AKI (intact tubular function)
questions.imageAlt: Renal biopsy showing widespread proximal tubular necrosis with sparing of distal tubules
questions.imageCaption: H&E: proximal tubular necrosis with distal tubule sparing — nephrotoxic ATN (heavy metal exposure)

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man undergoes renal biopsy for evaluation of chronic renal failure. The patient is obese and admits to smoking two packs per day for 30 years. Physical examination reveals a blood pressure of 190/110 mm Hg. An echocardiogram shows conspicuous left ventricular hypertrophy. A renal biopsy discloses pathologic changes in small renal arteries, including 'onion-skinning' and fibrinoid necrosis. The Congo red stain is negative. Laboratory studies show BUN of 45 mg/dL, and serum creatinine of 5.5 mg/dL. Which of the following is the most likely underlying cause of chronic renal failure in this patient?
questions.explanation: Malignant hypertension refers to severely elevated blood pressure that results in rapidly progressive vascular disease affecting the brain, heart, and kidney. The key histologic findings are 'onion-skinning' (concentric smooth muscle cell hyperplasia in small arteries) and fibrinoid necrosis — hallmarks that distinguish malignant from benign hypertension. Malignant hypertension injures endothelial cells and causes increased vascular permeability, leading to fibrinoid necrosis and smooth muscle proliferation. The Congo red stain is negative, excluding amyloid (choice A). Left ventricular hypertrophy indicates longstanding hypertension. Chronic pyelonephritis (choice B) causes cortical scarring, not onion-skin arteriopathy. Congestive heart failure (choice C) and Cushing syndrome (choice D) do not produce onion-skinning of renal arteries.
keypoints: ⭐ Malignant hypertension: 'onion-skinning' (hyperplastic arteriolosclerosis) + fibrinoid necrosis of arterioles = distinguishing histologic features
Benign nephrosclerosis: hyaline arteriolosclerosis (PAS-positive deposits); Malignant: onion-skin + fibrinoid necrosis
Clinical: severely elevated BP (>180/120) + papilledema + renal failure + microangiopathic hemolytic anemia
