Malignant HTN mechanism: endothelial injury → plasma protein insudation into vessel wall → fibrinoid necrosis → MAHA

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man with a history of hyperlipidemia and high blood pressure presents to the emergency room for evaluation of headaches and blurred vision. His blood pressure is 200/115 mm Hg, and pulse is 95 per minute. Funduscopic examination reveals several small retinal microaneurysms and cotton-like zones of retinal edema and necrosis. Intravenous pyelography discloses small kidneys bilaterally. Renal arteriography shows stenoses of both renal arteries. Hypertension in this patient is caused by the renal release of which of the following hormones?
questions.explanation: Renal artery stenosis causes ischemia to the juxtaglomerular (JG) apparatus, which responds by releasing renin. Renin cleaves angiotensinogen (produced by the liver) to form angiotensin I, which is then converted by ACE in the pulmonary vasculature to angiotensin II. Angiotensin II is a potent vasoconstrictor and also stimulates the adrenal cortex to produce aldosterone, leading to sodium and water retention — producing renovascular hypertension. In unilateral stenosis, the renin level in the renal vein of the ischemic kidney is elevated while the contralateral kidney is suppressed. Renal artery stenosis is caused by atherosclerosis in adults (most common) and fibromuscular dysplasia in young women and children. Aldosterone (choice A), angiotensin (choice B), and plasminogen (choice D) are not synthesized by the kidney. EPO (choice C) stimulates erythropoiesis.
keypoints: ⭐ Renovascular hypertension: renal artery stenosis → ↓ renal perfusion → ↑ RENIN from JG cells → angiotensin II → vasoconstriction + aldosterone → Na/water retention → HTN
Adults: atherosclerosis (most common cause of renal artery stenosis); young women: fibromuscular dysplasia
Classic finding: bilateral small kidneys + renovascular bruits + hypokalemia (from hyperaldosteronism)
Treatment: ACE inhibitors effective (but contraindicated in bilateral stenosis — can cause acute renal failure)

questions.stem: A 6-year-old child develops fever, abdominal pain, and bloody diarrhea. Several other children in the neighborhood had similar symptoms. The common feature was traced to eating hamburgers at a fast food restaurant. The clinical course is complicated by the development of acute renal failure. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Hemolytic uremic syndrome (HUS) features microangiopathic hemolytic anemia, thrombocytopenia, and acute renal failure. It is the most common cause of acute renal failure in children. The major cause is Shiga toxin-producing strains of E. coli (most commonly O157:H7), which are ingested in contaminated undercooked ground beef. The toxin injures glomerular and renal arteriolar endothelial cells, triggering thrombotic microangiopathy — thrombosis of small vessels, mechanical fragmentation of red cells (schistocytes), and ischemic renal injury. The cluster of cases linked to fast food hamburgers is classic for E. coli O157:H7 outbreak. Postinfectious GN (choice A) follows streptococcal infection and does not cause acute renal failure. The remaining choices are systemic vasculitides not linked to food-borne illness.
keypoints: ⭐ HUS: bloody diarrhea (E. coli O157:H7) → Shiga toxin → endothelial injury → thrombotic microangiopathy → microangiopathic hemolytic anemia + thrombocytopenia + AKI
Most common cause of AKI in children; epidemic form (D+ HUS) linked to E. coli O157:H7 in contaminated beef
Lab: schistocytes on smear, ↑ LDH, ↓ haptoglobin, ↓ platelets, negative Coombs
Do NOT use antibiotics for E. coli O157:H7 bloody diarrhea — increases Shiga toxin release and HUS risk

questions.stem: A 5-year-old girl presents with the sudden onset of diffuse arthralgias and skin rash. Physical examination shows a violaceous maculopapular rash on the lower torso. Urinalysis discloses oliguria and 2+ hematuria. Urine cultures are negative. This child's clinical presentation is commonly associated with which of the following diseases?
questions.explanation: Henoch-Schönlein purpura (IgA vasculitis) is the most common type of childhood vasculitis and is caused by vascular localization of immune complexes containing predominantly IgA. The classic tetrad includes: palpable purpuric rash (lower extremities and buttocks), arthralgia/arthritis, abdominal pain (GI involvement), and renal disease (hematuria, proteinuria). The glomerular lesion is identical to that of IgA nephropathy (Berger disease) — mesangial IgA deposits. HUS (choice C) is caused by Shiga toxin-producing E. coli and features bloody diarrhea and AKI, but is not typically associated with purpuric rash and arthralgias. Goodpasture syndrome (choice B) does not cause rash. Berger disease (choice A) causes recurrent hematuria in adults without systemic vasculitis signs. Polyarteritis nodosa (choice E) spares small vessels and does not produce purpura.
keypoints: ⭐ Henoch-Schönlein purpura (IgA vasculitis): palpable purpura (lower extremities/buttocks) + arthralgia + abdominal pain + hematuria/nephritis — IgA-mediated small vessel vasculitis
Renal biopsy: identical to IgA nephropathy — mesangial IgA deposits + C3
Most common vasculitis in children; often follows URI; self-limiting in most cases
Differentiate from HUS: HUS = bloody diarrhea + MAHA + thrombocytopenia + AKI; HSP = purpura + arthralgia + abdominal pain + hematuria (no thrombocytopenia)

questions.stem: A 50-year-old man is found to have blood in his urine during a routine checkup. He is otherwise in excellent health, except for a mild microcytic, hypochromic anemia. An enlarged right kidney is found on X-ray examination, and CT scan reveals a renal mass of irregular shape, measuring 6 cm in diameter. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Renal cell carcinoma (RCC) is the most common cancer of the kidney, accounting for 90% of kidney cancers. The classic clinical triad is flank pain, hematuria, and a palpable abdominal mass, although the full triad is present in only 10% of cases. Incidental detection during imaging is increasingly common. RCC in a 50-year-old man with hematuria, an enlarged kidney, and a unilateral renal mass is the most likely diagnosis. The 5-year survival is 90% if the tumor has not extended beyond the renal capsule, but drops to 30% with distant metastases. Angiomyolipoma (choice A) is a benign tumor associated with tuberous sclerosis; it is often fat-containing on CT. Metastatic carcinoma (choice B) tends to produce bilateral renal involvement. Nephroblastoma and Wilms tumor (choices C, E) are pediatric tumors.
keypoints: ⭐ Renal cell carcinoma: most common renal malignancy in adults (90%); classic triad = hematuria + flank pain + palpable mass (only 10% have all three)
Clear cell RCC (most common subtype): glycogen-rich clear cytoplasm; VHL gene mutation (chromosome 3p)
Paraneoplastic syndromes: polycythemia (EPO), hypercalcemia (PTHrP), hypertension (renin), Stauffer syndrome (nonmetastatic hepatic dysfunction)
Spreads to: lungs (cannon-ball metastases), bones, liver, adrenals, brain; invades renal vein and IVC
questions.imageAlt: Gross nephrectomy specimen showing a large renal cell carcinoma with golden-yellow and hemorrhagic cut surface
questions.imageCaption: Nephrectomy specimen: golden-yellow mass with hemorrhage — renal cell carcinoma (clear cell type) arising from renal tubular epithelium

questions.stem: For the patient described in Question 49 (50-year-old man with renal cell carcinoma), a fine-needle aspiration of the renal mass shows glycogen-rich tumor cells. Molecular studies would most likely identify mutations in which of the following growth regulatory genes?
questions.explanation: Clear cell carcinoma is the most common type of RCC. The cytoplasm appears clear because it is rich in glycogen and fat, which are washed out during histologic processing. Loss of one allele of the VHL (von Hippel-Lindau) tumor suppressor gene on chromosome 3p occurs in virtually all (98%) sporadic clear cell RCC, and mutations in the VHL gene are found in more than half of these tumors. VHL protein normally targets HIF-1α for degradation; loss of VHL leads to HIF-1α accumulation and upregulation of VEGF (angiogenesis) and PDGF — explaining the highly vascular nature of clear cell RCC. WT1 (choice E) is a tumor suppressor gene implicated in Wilms tumor, not adult RCC. IGF-2 (choice B) is associated with Wilms tumor in Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome. ADPKD (choice A) and PAX6 (choice C) are unrelated to RCC.
keypoints: ⭐ Clear cell RCC: VHL gene mutation (chromosome 3p) → ↑HIF-1α → ↑VEGF → highly vascular tumor; glycogen-rich clear cells
VHL syndrome: bilateral clear cell RCC + hemangioblastomas (cerebellum, retina) + pheochromocytoma + renal cysts
WT1 = Wilms tumor suppressor; WT1 mutations associated with WAGR syndrome (Wilms + Aniridia + GU anomalies + mental Retardation)
VEGF pathway mutations in RCC → therapeutic target: sunitinib, sorafenib (VEGF receptor inhibitors)

questions.stem: The mother of a 12-month-old boy palpates a mass on the right side of the infant's abdomen. The surgical specimen shows a large renal mass. Microscopically, the tumor is composed of multiple elements, including blastemal, stromal, and epithelial tissues. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Wilms tumor (nephroblastoma) is a malignant neoplasm of embryonal nephrogenic elements composed of elements resembling normal fetal tissue: metanephric blastema, immature stroma (mesenchymal tissue), and immature epithelial elements — the classic 'triphasic' histology. It is the most frequent abdominal solid tumor in children, with a prevalence of 1 in 10,000. Wilms tumor usually presents between 1 and 3 years of age, and 98% occur before 10 years of age. Most are sporadic and unilateral, but 5% arise in the context of congenital syndromes (WAGR, Beckwith-Wiedemann, Denys-Drash). Ganglioneuroma (choice A) and neuroblastoma (choice B) arise from neural/adrenal tissue and do not have triphasic nephron-like histology. RCC (choice C) is a tumor of adults. Teratocarcinoma (choice D) is a germ cell tumor.
keypoints: ⭐ Wilms tumor: triphasic histology (blastema + stroma + epithelial elements) in child 1-3 years with unilateral abdominal mass
Most common abdominal malignancy in children; presents as large, smooth, well-encapsulated flank mass
Associated syndromes: WAGR (WT1 deletion), Beckwith-Wiedemann (WT2/IGF-2, associated with hemihypertrophy)
Excellent prognosis with surgery + chemotherapy ± radiation; >90% survival for localized disease
questions.imageAlt: Gross surgical specimen of a large pediatric renal tumor showing variegated cut surface with hemorrhage and necrosis
questions.imageCaption: Nephrectomy specimen: large, variegated renal mass — Wilms tumor (nephroblastoma) with triphasic histology

questions.stem: A 75-year-old homeless man is brought to the emergency room in a coma. Upon admission to the hospital, the BUN is 74 mg/dL, and the creatinine is 6.5 mg/dL. He dies thereafter, and an autopsy reveals bilaterally enlarged kidneys with a dilated pelvis and calyces and severely atrophied renal parenchyma. The pathogenesis of this disease is most likely related to which of the following?
questions.explanation: Obstructive uropathy is caused by structural or functional abnormalities in the urinary tract that impede urine flow. In this neglected elderly patient, severe prostatic hyperplasia most likely caused chronic urinary tract obstruction, leading to hydronephrosis — progressive dilation of the renal pelves and calyces with atrophy of the renal parenchyma. The key gross feature is dilated collecting system (pelvis and calyces) with thinned, compressed cortex. Early microscopic findings include dilation of collecting ducts, followed by tubular dilation and progressive parenchymal atrophy. Polycystic kidney disease (choice C) produces large cystic kidneys but does not feature calyceal dilation. Systemic hypertension (choice D) causes uniformly small kidneys (nephrosclerosis). Amyloidosis (choice B) enlarges kidneys but does not cause hydronephrosis.
keypoints: ⭐ Hydronephrosis: urinary tract obstruction → dilation of renal pelvis and calyces → progressive parenchymal compression and atrophy
Common causes: BPH (elderly men), kidney stones, ureteral strictures, pelvic malignancy (cervical cancer), retroperitoneal fibrosis
Bilateral hydronephrosis → obstructive cause must be distal to bladder (BPH, neurogenic bladder) or bilateral (retroperitoneal mass)
Distinguish from polycystic kidney: PKD = multiple cysts; hydronephrosis = dilated pelvis/calyces with thinned parenchyma

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man dies of chronic renal failure. Examination of his kidneys at autopsy reveals a 'staghorn' calculus. Which of the following best describes the pathogenesis of this renal stone?
questions.explanation: Staghorn calculi (struvite stones, magnesium ammonium phosphate) are formed in the presence of urea-splitting bacteria (most commonly Proteus mirabilis, but also Klebsiella, Pseudomonas, and Staphylococcus). These bacteria split urea → ammonia, making the urine alkaline, which favors precipitation of magnesium ammonium phosphate (struvite) and calcium phosphate (apatite). The resulting calculi grow to fill the entire renal pelvis and calyces, creating the antler-like 'staghorn' shape. Gout (choice A) and hyperuricemia produce uric acid stones. Hereditary cystinuria (choice B) produces hexagonal cystine stones. Hypercalcemia (choice C) and hyperparathyroidism (choice D) cause calcium oxalate or phosphate stones, not staghorn calculi.
keypoints: ⭐ Staghorn calculi = struvite (magnesium ammonium phosphate) = infection stones; caused by urease-producing bacteria (Proteus, Klebsiella)
Mechanism: urease splits urea → ammonia → alkaline urine → struvite precipitation → fill entire pelvis ('staghorn')
Calcium oxalate = most common US stone (80%); associated with hypercalciuria, hyperoxaluria, hypocitraturia
Uric acid stones = gout, high cell turnover (leukemia, chemotherapy); radiolucent on plain X-ray
questions.imageAlt: Gross specimen of kidney with a large branching staghorn calculus filling the renal pelvis and calyces
questions.imageCaption: Gross kidney: staghorn calculus filling the entire collecting system — struvite (infection) stone from urease-producing bacteria

questions.stem: A 36-year-old woman presents with advanced cervical carcinoma, and a CT scan shows widespread pelvic spread. If this condition is not surgically corrected, the patient's kidneys will most likely develop which of the following conditions?
questions.explanation: Obstructive uropathy is caused by structural or functional abnormalities in the urinary tract that impede urine flow. Metastatic cervical cancer is a frequent cause of bilateral ureteral obstruction due to direct tumor compression or encasement of the ureters in the pelvis — producing bilateral hydronephrosis (dilation of the collecting system with progressive parenchymal atrophy). This is a common and serious complication of advanced pelvic malignancies. Polycystic kidney disease (choice C) is a congenital disease unrelated to obstruction. Acute vasculitis (choice A) and tubulointerstitial nephritis (choice E) are inflammatory processes not caused by ureteral compression. Staghorn calculi (choice D) are caused by urease-producing bacterial infections.
keypoints: ⭐ Advanced pelvic malignancy (cervical, bladder, prostate, colorectal) → bilateral ureteral obstruction → bilateral hydronephrosis → obstructive uropathy → post-renal AKI/CKD
Cervical cancer: most common gynecologic malignancy to cause bilateral ureteral obstruction
Ureteral stenting or nephrostomy tubes can temporize obstruction and preserve renal function
Bilateral ureteral obstruction → anuria; unilateral obstruction → may be asymptomatic if contralateral kidney is normal

questions.stem: A 34-year-old man undergoing cisplatin-based chemotherapy complains of a 1-week history of increasing fatigue and headaches. He also reports seeing blood in his urine. Blood pressure is 150/100 mm Hg. Physical examination reveals diffuse purpura over his upper trunk and arms. Laboratory studies show elevated levels of BUN and creatinine, and 24-hour urinalysis reveals hematuria and oliguria. Urine cultures are negative. A CBC demonstrates severe anemia (hematocrit 28%) and thrombocytopenia (50,000/μL). The direct Coombs test is negative. A peripheral blood smear reveals schistocytes. Which of the following is the most likely cause of acute renal failure in this patient?
questions.explanation: Thrombotic microangiopathy (TMA) has a variety of causes, all of which cause endothelial damage that initiates a final common pathway of vascular thrombosis and ischemia. Cisplatin chemotherapy is a recognized cause of TMA. The presentation here — microangiopathic hemolytic anemia (schistocytes on smear, Coombs-negative hemolysis), thrombocytopenia, and acute renal failure — constitutes the classic triad of thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (TTP)/HUS-like syndrome. Cisplatin injures endothelial cells, promoting thrombosis in glomerular and arteriolar capillaries. The renal pathology is comparable to malignant hypertensive nephropathy with fibrinoid necrosis and arteriolar thrombosis. Nephrotoxic ATN (choice C) does not cause thrombocytopenia or schistocytes. HSP (choice B) does not have microangiopathic features. ANCA GN (choice A) and PAN (choice D) are vasculitides without schistocytes.
keypoints: ⭐ Thrombotic microangiopathy (TMA) triad: MAHA (schistocytes, Coombs-negative) + thrombocytopenia + AKI; Coombs-negative = not autoimmune hemolysis
Causes: E. coli O157:H7 (HUS in children), ADAMTS13 deficiency (TTP in adults), cisplatin/mitomycin, malignant HTN, pregnancy (HELLP)
TTP vs HUS: TTP = neurologic predominance; HUS = renal predominance (but overlap exists)
Treatment: plasma exchange (plasmapheresis) for TTP; supportive + dialysis for HUS

questions.stem: A 16-year-old black girl with sickle cell anemia presents to the emergency room because she is experiencing severe bone pain (avascular necrosis). An abdominal CT scan shows evidence of splenic infarcts. Which of the following renal diseases is a direct complication of this patient's vasoocclusive disease?
questions.explanation: In sickle cell disease, the rigidity of sickled erythrocytes causes obstruction of the microcirculation with subsequent hypoxia and ischemic injury. The renal medulla and papillae are particularly vulnerable because the vasa recta — the capillaries coursing through the medulla — have a low oxygen tension, high osmolality, and slow blood flow that promotes sickling. Erythrocyte sickling in the vasa recta occludes their lumina, causing medullary and papillary infarcts — papillary necrosis. This is the most common direct renal complication of sickle cell disease. Chronic sickle cell nephropathy manifests as hematuria, inability to concentrate urine (isosthenuria), and, in severe cases, renal failure. Acute pyelonephritis (choice A) is not a direct consequence of vasoocclusion. Urate nephropathy (choice D) and urolithiasis (choice E) are not direct complications of sickle cell anemia. Polycystic kidney disease (choice C) is a congenital condition.
keypoints: ⭐ Sickle cell nephropathy: vasa recta sickling → papillary infarction → PAPILLARY NECROSIS → hematuria + impaired concentrating ability
Other conditions causing papillary necrosis: mnemonic POSTCARD — Pyelonephritis, Obstruction, Sickle cell, TB, Cirrhosis/alcoholism, Analgesics (NSAIDs/phenacetin), Renal vein thrombosis, Diabetes
Isosthenuria (fixed urine Osm ~300 mOsm/kg) = inability to concentrate or dilute urine due to medullary damage
Sickle cell also causes: gross hematuria, nephrotic syndrome (FSGS), CKD

questions.stem: A 46-year-old man with no past medical history presents with excruciating episodic (colicky) right flank pain. A renal stone is passed. In the United States, this stone is most likely composed of which of the following?
questions.explanation: Calcium oxalate stones are the most common form of kidney stones in the United States, accounting for approximately 80% of all stones. Both calcium oxalate and calcium phosphate stones are usually related to idiopathic hypercalciuria and increased intestinal calcium absorption. Calcium oxalate stones are radiopaque, hard, and often have an irregular surface. Calcium phosphate stones (choice B) are more common in England. Magnesium ammonium phosphate/struvite stones (choice D) are associated with urease-producing bacterial infections and form staghorn calculi. Uric acid stones (choice E) are smooth, yellow, hard, and radiolucent; found in 25% of patients with gout. Cystine stones (choice C) occur in children with hereditary cystinuria, an inborn error of amino acid transport.
keypoints: ⭐ Kidney stone composition (US): Calcium oxalate 80% >> uric acid ~10% > struvite ~5% > cystine ~1%
Calcium oxalate: hard, radiopaque, associated with hypercalciuria, hyperoxaluria, hypocitraturia (citrate is a natural inhibitor)
Uric acid: radiolucent, yellow, associated with gout or high purine diet; can be dissolved with urinary alkalinization
Cystine: hexagonal crystals on UA; hereditary defect in dibasic amino acid transporter (cystine, ornithine, arginine, lysine = COAL)

questions.stem: A 12-year-old girl complains of headaches and blurred vision. She has a history of high blood pressure, but is not currently taking medication. Her blood pressure is 160/95 mm Hg and pulse is 95 per minute. Funduscopic examination reveals small retinal microaneurysms and cotton-like zones of retinal edema and necrosis. She is hospitalized for further evaluation. Renal arteriography shows segmental stenoses forming multiple ridges that project into the lumen. What is the most likely cause of secondary hypertension in this young patient?
questions.explanation: Fibromuscular dysplasia is the most frequent cause of renovascular hypertension in children and young women. It is characterized by fibrous and muscular stenosis of the renal artery, with areas of medial thickening alternating with areas of atrophy, producing the classic 'string of beads' pattern on angiogram — segmental stenoses forming ridges that project into the lumen. Stenosis reduces renal perfusion, triggering renin-mediated renovascular hypertension. Reconstitution of the arterial lumen (via balloon angioplasty) can be curative. Buerger disease (choice A) and Kawasaki disease (choice D) do not typically affect the renal arteries. Giant cell arteritis (choice C) and Takayasu arteritis (choice E) may cause renal artery stenosis, but are distinctly uncommon in children and do not produce the 'string of beads' pattern.
keypoints: ⭐ Fibromuscular dysplasia: 'string of beads' on angiography = alternating medial thickening + atrophy → segmental stenoses → renovascular HTN
Demographics: young women and children; contrast with atherosclerotic renal artery stenosis (elderly men)
Mechanism: stenosis → ↓ renal perfusion → ↑ renin → angiotensin II → hypertension
Treatment: percutaneous transluminal angioplasty (PTA) is often curative; ACE inhibitors for medical management
